clinical_presentation: Aphasia (difficulty with speech or language comprehension)
Right homonymous hemianopsia (loss of vision in the right half of the visual field)

differential_diagnoses.justification: This is the explicit radiological impression provided in the report, consistent with the described gyriform T2/FLAIR hyperintensities.
Can present with similar gyriform cortical T2/FLAIR hyperintensities and infarction due to venous congestion, especially if arterial imaging appears normal.
Transient gyriform cortical FLAIR hyperintensities can be seen following seizures, mimicking ischemic changes, although the term 'infarction' implies permanent damage.

management_plan.immediate_actions: Clinical assessment to determine acuity of symptoms, comprehensive neurological examination, vital signs monitoring, and initiation of a full stroke workup.
further_investigations: Blood tests (e.g., CBC, electrolytes, renal/liver function, glucose, lipids, coagulation profile)
ECG and Holter monitor (for cardiac arrhythmias)
MRA/CTA of brain and neck vessels
management_plan.follow_up_timeline: Urgent clinical review within days for comprehensive stroke workup and initiation of secondary prevention strategies.

parsed.patient_explanation: Your brain scan shows some areas on the left side of your brain, specifically in the front, side, and back regions, where the blood supply was reduced. This has caused a small area of damage, which we call an ischemic cortical infarction, essentially a type of stroke. We also noted some inflammation in your left sinus, which is a separate common finding. We need to investigate the cause of this stroke and plan how to prevent any future events.

triage_priority.justification: The finding of an ischemic cortical infarction requires urgent clinical correlation and comprehensive workup to determine its acuity, underlying cause, and to initiate secondary prevention strategies.
